subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] mm: kick writeback flusher for IOCB_DONTCACHE with targeted dirty tracking

2026-05-01.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's writeback behavior to improve performance in the IOCB_DONTCACHE path. The original code submitted writeback requests inline, causing significant latency and contention. This patch introduces a background flusher that writes back dirty pages in the background, reducing the impact on the writer's hot path. To limit unnecessary flushing, it uses a dedicated bit and counter to target only the necessary dirty pages. The result is improved throughput and reduced tail latency for sequential write workloads.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch description should include a Reviewed-by tag from themselves, and added their own Reviewed-by line to the comment

Jan Kara pointed out that the patch description inaccurately states that only dontcache pages are written back, when in fact arbitrary dirty pages are written back based on the WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY counter.
reviews.raw_body:
Nice and looks good to me now. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

One nit below:

---

This comment is a bit confusing as in fact we write arbitrary dirty pages.
It is only the amount of pages that is influenced by WB_DONTCACHE_DIRTY. So
I'd rephrase the last sentence like: We queue writeback for the inode's wb
for as many pages as there are dontcache pages but we don't restrict
writeback to dontcache pages only. This significantly improves performance
over either writing all wb's pages or writing only dontcache pages.
Although it doesn't guarantee quick writeback and reclaim of dontcache
pages it keeps the amount of dirty pages in check and over longer term
dontcache pages get written and reclaimed by background writeback even with
this rough heuristic.

								Honza

2026-05-03.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's writeback behavior to improve performance in the IOCB_DONTCACHE path. The original code submitted writeback requests inline, causing significant latency and contention. This patch introduces a background flusher that writes back dirty pages in the background, reducing the impact on the writer's hot path. To limit unnecessary flushing, it uses a dedicated bit and counter to target only the necessary dirty pages. The result is improved throughput and reduced tail latency for sequential write workloads.
